title: Polenta Morbida
subtitle: Soft, creamy polenta with Parmesan and butter: Northern Italy's most comforting side
sourceNotes: Polenta has been the staple grain of Northern Italy since maize arrived from the Americas in the sixteenth century. The morbida (soft) preparation is standard in Lombardy, Veneto, and Friuli. Pellegrino Artusi's La Scienza in Cucina (1891) contains detailed polenta instructions consistent with modern technique.

content.text: takes time. The forty-minute cook is not optional. It is what transforms raw, starchy grain into something smooth and deeply savoury. Polenta is forgiving of inattention (stir every few minutes rather than constantly after the first five), but it rewards the time investment.

Bring {{water}} of water to the boil in a large, heavy-based pan. Add 1.5 tsp of salt. Reduce the heat to medium-low. Pour {{polenta-medium-ground}} of polenta into the water in a slow, steady stream, whisking constantly with your other hand. Switch to a wooden spoon once all the polenta is in. Stir continuously for the first 5 minutes as it thickens.

Reduce to the lowest heat. Cook for 35 to 40 minutes, stirring every 4 to 5 minutes and scraping the bottom of the pan each time to prevent any scorching. The polenta is ready when it pulls away cleanly from the sides and a trail left by the spoon holds its shape for a few seconds.

Remove from the heat. Add {{butter-unsalted}} of cold butter in cubes and {{parmesan}} of grated Parmesan. Beat vigorously until both are fully incorporated and the polenta is glossy. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve immediately, polenta stiffens quickly as it cools. A splash of hot water and a vigorous stir restores it if needed.

Polenta morbida is most commonly served as the base for something braised or saucy: sautéed mushrooms, a slow-cooked ragu, braised sausages, or chicken alla cacciatora. Spread it in a wide, shallow bowl and ladle the topping over. It is also excellent cold, left to set in a tin, sliced, and pan-fried in butter until golden on both sides.
